You can set the LLM to always take the highest probability option, but this leads to very rigid behavior - the thing gets stuck in local minima. Letting it pick from the top 2-4 responses allows it to escape this and find a more globally optimal response. In theory.

How does an LLM work? An LLM is a black box. We know why it works the way it does- the principles behind it- but we can't take the sides off and look inside it and go "ah, here's the bit that makes it say 'you're absolutely right and I'm sorry.'" We do know it doesn't have any facts inside it.

One of the reasons it seems to produce mostly workable code isn't because it knoes how to code- remember that it doesn't know anything. But code is a kind of very specific language that there are a lot of educational examples for, so the probabilities in the LLM produce mostly-plausible code.

Human language is full of lies and fictions. An LLM doesn't know that. It just holds probabilities of how often one word follows another, or another sequence. The only thing it can do is output something novel that is plausibly an answer to your prompt.

Remember this? This was a really good example of the prompt massage that occurs behind the scenes when you use an LLM. When someone entered "Homer Simpson surrounded by swords" their prompt was invisibly edited to include "if not otherwise specified, make all characters ethnically ambiguous"

Now, that part of the prompt massage was removed- but the prompt massage remains. Because we don't know how the LLM works exactly, we can't go inside and tinker with it. We can only add in a system prompt along with the user prompt that says things like "don't say how to make a pipe bomb"

You either rebuild the LLM but kore powerful and with more data and hope for the best, or you tinker with adding in a system prompt with the user prompt, or you edit by altering the output of the machine before showing it to the reader. That's the limit of the "improvements" to be made.

Right? It was an attempt to balance out the fact that they'd fed their LLM too many white faces, so it almost never produced non-white people in outputs unless you specified. So if you asked "fireman picture, cute" it would edit it to "ethnically ambiguous fireman picture, cute" before running it.

I reworked my input example to be "I'm such a Jim Davis fan my cat's name is". (In case you care, "Jim Davis" tokenizes more simply than "Hanna-Barbera" and is thus less visuallysemantically awkward and cluttered). To start here's the entire input passing through the entire LLM (Llama 3.2 3B)

Every layer of a transformer LLM has multiple parallel attention heads (this LLM has 24). Each head is attending inputs to other inputs in different ways. Not as simple as saying head 3 attends adjectives to nouns and 7 does articles; how each head attends is however it optimized during training.

Here are the attention matrices for some of the attention heads in the first layer of this LLM while processing "I'm such a Jim Davis fan my cat's name is." The inputs along the top are attending to the inputs on the side. 2 things of note here. 1: a lot of the attention is on the diagonal.

Let's jump ahead to layer 3. By this point attention is starting to get more diffuse and you can start to see "cat's name is" grouping together. If you look at the full flow through the LLM up top under "is", you can see that a couple of layers down the model starts anticipating a name next.

Then if you go up to the full LLM trace, right after L15 you can see where "is" has moved from "I'm anticipating the name of a cat" to "I'm anticipating the name of a cat related to a Jim Davis cartooncomic" and then the last few layers are it pulling increasingly towards Garfield (well, "Gar").

So anyway, that's attention, which is the critical component of transformer-based LLMs (the seminal paper that started all this LLM stuff was literally titled "Attention Is All You Need"). If you want to talk about the good, the bad, andor the ugly of LLM architecture, that's where it is.

Having crossed paths with attention mechanisms in lots of ways, my simplest take is that they are both stunningly robust and frustratingly fragile. They're behind absolutely bonkers LLM responses as well as ones that make people feel like they're talking to something sentient that understands them.
